title: Classifier zoo + experiment runner
description:
## Complexity
Medium (multiple PyTorch classifier architectures, config-driven training)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Implement a zoo of classifier architectures that all consume frozen modality embeddings. A config-driven experiment runner trains any architecture on any target and saves results.

### Architectures
1. SingleModalityClassifier: MLP on one embedding (text-only, stats-only, GIN-only baselines)
2. StackedClassifier: Concat all selected modalities -> MLP (current R2/R3 approach)
3. GatedFusionClassifier: Learn per-modality attention weights, then concat weighted embeddings -> MLP
4. LateFusionClassifier: Separate MLP per modality -> average logits (ensemble)

All classifiers share the same interface: __init__(modality_dims, n_classes, hidden), forward(embeddings) -> logits.

### Experiment runner
- Config dataclass: target, modalities, classifier_arch, hidden_dim, lr, seed, max_epochs
- Training loop: standard PyTorch with early stopping on val F1
- Saves: best model weights, training curves, per-example test predictions, metrics JSON
- Results directory: results/fusion/{target}/{arch}_{modalities}_{timestamp}.json

### Implementation
1. Create classification/fusion/ — new package
   - classification/fusion/models.py — all classifier architectures
   - classification/fusion/train.py — generic training loop
   - classification/fusion/run.py — config-driven experiment runner
   - classification/fusion/config.py — ExperimentConfig dataclass
2. Delete or archive old classification/route3.py (conflated GIN+classifier)

## Acceptance criteria
- All 4 architectures implemented and trainable
- Experiment runner can sweep: 4 archs x 2 targets x N modality combos
- Each run produces: model .pt, training curves .png, predictions .npy, metrics .json
- At least one full sweep completed: all architectures on AI adoption with all 3 modalities

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW
ISSUE: Sweep scope is ambiguous. Acceptance says 'at least one full sweep completed: all architectures on AI adoption with all 3 modalities.' But 'all 3 modalities' could mean: (a) text+stats+GIN for fusion only (1 combo), (b) all 6 combos for AI adoption (24 experiments = 6 combos x 4 archs), or (c) all 12 combos across both targets (48 experiments).
QUESTION: What is the minimum sweep for this bead to be considered complete?
SUGGESTED FIX: Pin the scope: 'Minimum sweep: all 4 architectures on AI adoption with 4 modality combos (text-only, text+stats, text+GIN, text+stats+GIN) = 16 experiments. Cohort target and single-modality baselines for stats/GIN are stretch.'

title: [epic] Target-Agnostic Modality Fusion
description:
## Epic
Test whether graph and text modalities carry complementary signal when both are represented as target-agnostic, frozen embeddings.

## Architecture principle
Encoders produce fixed vectors. Only classifiers learn per-task.

text -> [SBERT, frozen] -> 768-dim
stats -> [deterministic] -> 30-dim
graph -> [GIN, frozen] -> 128-dim

All three feed into a classifier. Only the classifier learns per target.

## Why this matters
The current GIN is trained end-to-end with classification loss. Its embedding IS task-specific. You cannot evaluate whether graph modality adds signal when the graph encoder was trained to solve the same task. By freezing both encoders, the classifier becomes the only locus of task learning.

## Experiment matrix
3 fixed representations x 4 classifier architectures x 2 targets (AI adoption, cohort):
- Single-modality: Linear/MLP on one embedding
- Stacked: Concat all modalities -> linear/MLP (current R2/R3 approach)
- Gated fusion: Learn per-modality weights -> classifier
- Late fusion: Separate classifiers -> ensemble vote

## Gate
Report answers: (a) With target-agnostic encoders, does graph modality add signal over text alone? (b) Does gated fusion outperform stacked concatenation? (c) Does the answer differ by target?

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: ✓ Mostly clear
MINOR: Experiment matrix says '3 fixed representations x 4 classifier architectures x 2 targets' but the actual number of combinations needed is ambiguous. The ENGINEERING.md says 48 experiments (2x6x4), but the epic says 3x4x2. Which modality combos are required vs optional?
SUGGESTED FIX: Clarify in epic: 'Required: text-only, text+stats, text+GIN for all 4 archs on AI adoption (12 experiments). Optional: cohort target, text+stats+GIN combo.'

title: GIN autoencoder — self-supervised graph encoder
description:
## Complexity
Medium (modify GIN training to use self-supervised objective)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Replace the current task-supervised GIN training with a self-supervised objective that produces a target-agnostic graph encoder. The encoder learns to represent graph structure without any classification labels.

### Architecture (Option A — node type reconstruction autoencoder)
- Encoder: 2-layer GIN (388-dim node features -> 256 hidden -> 128 output) + global mean pool
- Decoder head: Linear(128, 4) — predicts node type (Construct/Value/Stance/CSM) from per-node embeddings
- Loss: Cross-entropy on 4-class node type prediction (NOT edge prediction — see below)
- Train on ALL 1,250 graphs (no labels needed, no train/val/test split for encoder training)

### Why node-type-only (not node-types + edges)
Edge prediction on 15-node graphs has 87% negative class (non-edges), allowing trivial 87% accuracy without learning structure. Node types require neighborhood information — a node's type depends on what it connects to. This forces the encoder to learn structural patterns without the class-imbalance problem.

### Option C — contrastive variant (future)
Graph contrastive learning (corrupt graph, train encoder to distinguish original vs corrupted) is a viable alternative. This can be added as a separate encoder (`encoding/gnn/contrastive.py`) producing a second 128-dim frozen embedding for comparison. Not required for this bead — the experiment matrix uses the autoencoder (Option A) embedding.

### Implementation
1. Create encoding/gnn/autoencoder.py — GIN encoder + node type decoder, self-supervised training loop
2. Train on all 1,250 graphs, save encoder weights to cache/gin_encoder.pt
3. Create encoding/gnn/encode.py — load frozen encoder, produce 128-dim embeddings with cache-first pattern (matching text_encoder.py)
4. Move old encoding/gnn/model.py and encoding/gnn/train.py to encoding/gnn/_archived/ with README explaining deprecation
5. Defer route3.py cleanup to Bead C (olc)

## Acceptance criteria
- GIN autoencoder trains successfully on 1,250 graphs (converging node-type reconstruction loss)
- Node type prediction accuracy reported as training quality metric
- Frozen encoder produces 128-dim embeddings for any graph set
- Embeddings cached to cache/gin_embeddings.npy with ID index (cache-first, like text/stats)
- Old model.py and train.py moved to encoding/gnn/_archived/ with deprecation README
- Training curves saved to cache/gin_autoencoder_curves.png

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW
ISSUE 1: Loss function conflict. Bead says 'node type reconstruction loss + edge prediction loss (binary cross-entropy on adjacency)' but ENGINEERING.md specifies only node type reconstruction (cross-entropy on 4-class). Two developers would implement different autoencoders.
QUESTION: Should the autoencoder use (a) node type reconstruction only, or (b) node type + edge prediction? (a) is simpler and matches ENGINEERING.md.
SUGGESTED FIX: Align bead and ENGINEERING.md. Recommend node-type-only (simpler, faster to converge, and the ENGINEERING.md spec is more recent).

ISSUE 2: Scope includes 'Delete or archive old model.py and train.py' AND 'Update classification/route3.py.' But Bead C already handles route3.py deletion. The old encoding files are in encoding-specialist's domain, but their deletion is cleanup, not the core deliverable.
SUGGESTED FIX: Remove 'Update classification/route3.py' (defer to Bead C). Keep 'Archive old model.py and train.py' but make it a separate acceptance criterion: 'Old files moved to encoding/gnn/_archived/ with README explaining deprecation.'

title: Confirmatory: H1-H4 structural analysis by cohort
description:
## Complexity
Medium (statistical tests on existing graph data, no new extraction)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Test the four pre-registered structural hypotheses against 1,250 canonical concept graphs.

### H1 — Scientist hub-and-spoke
Scientists exhibit higher Construct:Value ratio than other cohorts, with Value nodes serving as high-degree hubs.
- **Metric**: Construct:Value ratio per graph, mean Value degree centrality
- **Test**: Kruskal-Wallis (non-parametric, distributions may be non-normal) + Dunn post-hoc with Bonferroni
- **Expected**: Scientists > Workforce; Scientists > Creatives

### H2 — Creative negative valence
Creatives exhibit higher proportion of negative-valence Stances.
- **Metric**: Fraction of Stances with negative valence per graph
- **Test**: Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn post-hoc
- **Expected**: Creatives > Workforce; Creatives > Scientists

### H3 — Workforce bipolarity
Workforce respondents exhibit higher bipolarity completeness scores.
- **Metric**: Mean bipolarity_complete per Construct per graph
- **Test**: Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn post-hoc
- **Expected**: Workforce > Creatives

### H4 — Scientist cognitive style
CSM markers more prevalent in scientist transcripts, more likely verification-oriented.
- **Metric**: CSM count per graph, fraction of verification-oriented CSM subtypes
- **Test**: Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn post-hoc
- **Expected**: Scientists > Workforce; Scientists > Creatives

## Implementation
1. Create `notebooks/04_structural_analysis.py` (Marimo notebook)
2. Load canonical graphs + demographic labels
3. Compute metrics per graph
4. Run statistical tests with Bonferroni correction
5. Render violin/box plots per hypothesis
6. Write findings to results-log.md

## Acceptance criteria
- All 4 hypotheses tested with appropriate statistics
- Effect sizes reported (Cliff's delta or eta-squared)
- Bonferroni-corrected significance thresholds
- Plots rendered
- Results-log.md updated with H1-H4 outcomes
close_reason: Created notebooks/04_structural_analysis.py with H1-H4 statistical tests. H2 supported, H1 significant but reversed, H3/H4 undermined by ceiling effects. Results-log.md updated.

title: Phase 4 — Structural Analysis (RQ2)
description:
## Epic
Test structural hypotheses about concept graph topology across professional cohorts (confirmatory, pre-registered H1-H4) and AI adoption levels (exploratory, new target from demographic extraction).

## Scope
### Confirmatory — cohort-based (H1-H4)
- H1: Scientist hub-and-spoke (Construct:Value ratio, Value degree centrality)
- H2: Creative negative valence (Stance valence distribution)
- H3: Workforce bipolarity (Construct bipolarity completeness)
- H4: Scientist cognitive style (CSM prevalence, verification-orientation)

### Exploratory — AI adoption-based
- Structural differences between tool_user and integrated graphs
- AI-concept subgraph analysis: are AI concepts central or peripheral?
- Same metrics as H1-H4, grouped by AI adoption instead of cohort

## Gate
All hypotheses tested with appropriate statistics (ANOVA/Kruskal-Wallis + post-hoc with Bonferroni correction). Results logged to results-log.md. Phase 1 post-mortem created.

## Notes
- No new extraction or model training — pure analysis on existing canonical graphs + demographic labels
- Pre-registered hypotheses (H1-H4) tested first, exploratory analysis clearly labelled
- Career stage excluded — too few labeled samples
close_reason: All Phase 4 tasks complete: H1-H4 confirmatory analysis, AI adoption exploratory analysis, Phase 1 post-mortem, Phase 4 synthesis.

title: Classify demographic targets: AI adoption (binary) and career stage (3-class)
description:
## Complexity
Medium (task loaders, 5 routes x 2 targets, comparison tables)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Context
Demographic extraction complete (bead sfz). Two classification targets:

**AI adoption** — binary (n=1,224): tool_user (602) vs integrated (622). novice (21) and power_user (5) dropped.
**Career stage** — 3-class (n=430): early (105) / mid (264) / late (61). uncertain (820) dropped.

## Implementation
1. Create classification/demographic_tasks.py — loads demographics, creates stratified splits, evaluates 5 routes per target
2. Print comparison tables
3. Update results-log.md

## Research questions
- Does graph modality add more value on these targets than on cohort?
- Does R3b (GIN-only) beat text-only?
- Is career stage encoded in graph topology?

## Acceptance criteria
- Both targets evaluated on all 5 routes
- Test macro-F1 + per-class F1 reported
- Delta vs baseline computed
- Results-log.md updated

title: Full-scale demographic extraction on all 1,250 transcripts
description:
## Complexity
Medium (run extraction script, handle rate limits, validate output)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Context
DeepSeek was selected over Agnes in the 20-transcript test (bead `lxk`). It follows the prompt's 'uncertain is safe' instruction faithfully and makes finer-grained AI adoption distinctions. Now we run it on all 1,250 transcripts.

## Implementation
1. Write `extraction/demographics_extractor.py` — loads human-only text for each transcript, calls DeepSeek API with prompt v2, caches results
2. Cache path: `cache/demographics.jsonl` (one JSON object per line, keyed by transcript_id)
3. Handle rate limits: 1 request/sec, retry with exponential backoff
4. Cache-first: skip already-extracted transcripts on re-run
5. Validate output: check for missing transcripts, schema compliance, class distribution

## Acceptance criteria
- All 1,250 transcripts extracted
- Output cached at `cache/demographics.jsonl`
- Class distribution reported (career_stage and ai_adoption)
- No missing or malformed entries
- Cost logged

## Notes
- Prompt: `extraction/prompts/demographics_v1.txt` (v2)
- Model: deepseek-chat (OpenAI-compatible endpoint, JSON mode)
- Human-only text (not full formatted transcript)
- Estimated cost: ~\-3
- Estimated runtime: ~15-25 minutes at 1 req/sec

title: Re-encode with human-only text to remove interviewer confound
description:
## Complexity
Medium (modify encoder, re-run pipeline, re-evaluate)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Context
The current text embeddings use the full transcript (AI + Human turns). The AI interviewer uses cohort-specific scripts — "creative work" vs "scientific work" vs "work routines" — injecting the label into every transcript. This creates an artificial ceiling effect: the classifier achieves perfect F1 by spotting the interviewer's word choices, not the interviewee's cognitive patterns.

## Implementation
1. Add `speaker_filter` parameter to `text_encoder.py` (default `None` = all speakers, `"Human"` = human-only)
2. Delete cached embeddings (`cache/text_embeddings.npy`, `cache/text_embedding_ids.json`)
3. Re-encode 1,250 transcripts with `speaker_filter="Human"`
4. Retrain Route 1 (baseline): `uv run python classification/baseline.py`
5. Retrain Route 2 (text + stats): `uv run python classification/route2.py`
6. Retrain Route 3 (text + GIN): delete `cache/best_gin.pt`, `uv run python classification/route3.py`
7. Run test evaluation: `uv run python classification/test_evaluation.py`
8. Update results-log.md with new results

## Notes
- Graphs are NOT re-extracted — they contain some AI-turn content but the impact is second-order (conceptual topics, not surface keywords)
- All cached models will be regenerated
- Route 3 requires GNN retraining (~3-4 min for 50 epochs on CPU)

## Acceptance criteria
- `text_encoder.py` supports human-only encoding via parameter
- Route 1 val macro-F1 drops from 1.0 (confirming confound removal)
- Routes 2 and 3 can now potentially beat baseline
- All three routes re-evaluated on test set
- New comparison table saved with fresh timestamp

title: Create GNN training loop (encoding/gnn/train.py)
description:
## Complexity
Medium (PyTorch training loop with early stopping, lr scheduling, class weights)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Implement `encoding/gnn/train.py` — the GIN training loop. Must handle batched graph data via torch_geometric DataLoader, class-weighted loss, early stopping on val macro-F1, and model checkpointing.

### Implementation
- DataLoader with batch_size=32
- CrossEntropyLoss with class weights from inverse frequency
- Adam(lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)
- ReduceLROnPlateau(mode='max', patience=5, factor=0.5)
- Early stopping: patience=10 on val macro-F1
- Save best model to `cache/best_gin.pt`
- tqdm progress bar per epoch
- Log train loss, val loss, val macro-F1 per epoch
- Plot train/val loss and macro-F1 curves, save as `cache/gnn_curves.png`
- Train for max 50 epochs

### Tests (`tests/test_gnn_train.py`)
- Training loop runs without error on a small subset (10 graphs)
- Best model checkpoint saved to cache/
- Train/val curves plot saved
- Early stopping triggers before 50 epochs on small data
- Class weights computed correctly (higher weight for minority class)

### Acceptance criteria
- `encoding/gnn/train.py` created and functional
- Training loop runs on subset, produces checkpoint and plots
- Early stopping works (triggers before max epochs)
- Class weights are computed from training labels only
- All tests pass
- Ruff clean

title: Final evaluation on held-out test set
description:
## Complexity
Medium (evaluate all 3 routes on test set, produce comparison table)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Evaluate all three trained routes on the held-out test set. This is the ONLY time the test set is touched. Produce the final comparison table and save all results with timestamps.

### Implementation
1. Load all three trained models
2. Evaluate each on test set: macro-F1, per-class F1, confusion matrix
3. Produce comparison table: baseline vs route 2 vs route 3
4. Save results to `results/{route}_{timestamp}.json`
5. Update `results-log.md` with test set metrics
6. Render confusion matrix grid (3 routes × 1 figure)

### Acceptance criteria
- Test set evaluated exactly once
- Macro-F1 reported for all 3 routes on test set
- Δ vs baseline computed for routes 2 and 3
- Confusion matrices rendered for all 3 routes
- Results saved with timestamps (never overwritten)
- results-log.md updated

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text: CLARITY REVIEW: ✓ Clear. Test set discipline explicit, timestamped results, comparison table format specified.

title: Route 3 — Text + GIN graph embedding classifier
description:
## Complexity
High (GNN training loop, hyperparameter tuning on val, early stopping)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Train a 2-layer GIN encoder that produces 128-dim graph embeddings, then fuse with text embeddings (768-dim) and classify through a 2-layer MLP head. Uses the training loop from `encoding/gnn/train.py` (created in bead 3e-1). Class-weighted loss, early stopping on val macro-F1, lr scheduling.

### Implementation
1. Run `encoding/gnn/train.py` with canonicalised graphs via GraphDataset:
   - GIN encoder + classifier trained end-to-end
   - Best model saved to `cache/best_gin.pt`
   - Train/val curves saved to `cache/gnn_curves.png`
2. Evaluate on validation set
3. Compare Δ vs baseline and route 2

### Tests (`tests/test_route3.py`)
- GIN encoder produces 128-dim embeddings
- Training loop runs without error
- Best model checkpoint saved
- Validation macro-F1 is computed
- Early stopping triggers before max epochs

### Acceptance criteria
- GIN trained with early stopping
- Validation macro-F1 recorded
- Train/val curves plotted and saved to cache/gnn_curves.png
- Δ vs baseline and route 2 computed
- Best model saved to cache/best_gin.pt
- All tests pass

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW
ISSUE: 'Run encoding/gnn/train.py (create if needed)' — train.py does not exist. This defers a core implementation decision to the executor.
QUESTION: Should train.py creation be a separate bead, or is it part of this one? If part of this one, what is the exact architecture?
SUGGESTED FIX: Either (a) split into 3e-1 (create encoding/gnn/train.py) and 3e-2 (train and evaluate GNN), or (b) add explicit acceptance: 'encoding/gnn/train.py created with: DataLoader batch_size=32, train loop with tqdm progress, validation after each epoch, model checkpoint on best val F1, train/val curves saved as cache/gnn_curves.png'

title: Route 2 — Text + graph statistics classifier
description:
## Complexity
Medium (compute stats, concatenate, train LR, feature importance)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Train a logistic regression classifier on concatenated [text embedding (768-dim) + graph statistics (30-dim)]. Compute permutation feature importance to identify which graph properties drive classification.

### Implementation
1. Run `encoding/graph_stats.py` to compute 30-dim feature vectors for all canonicalised graphs
2. Concatenate: [text_embedding | graph_stats] = 798-dim per transcript
3. Train LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced', max_iter=1000, C=1.0) on train set
4. Evaluate on validation set: macro-F1, per-class F1, confusion matrix
5. Compute permutation importance for all 30 graph stat features
6. Compare Δ macro-F1 vs baseline

### Tests (`tests/test_route2.py`)
- Concatenated feature shape is (N, 798)
- Model trains without error
- Permutation importance returns 30 values
- Graph features are normalized to [0,1]
- Validation set not leaked

### Acceptance criteria
- 30-dim vectors computed for all 1,250 transcripts
- Route 2 classifier trained
- Validation macro-F1 recorded
- Δ vs baseline computed
- Top-10 permutation features printed as table with columns: rank, feature name, importance score, one-line description of what the feature measures (e.g. 'bipolarity_score: completeness of construct bipolarity across graph')
- All tests pass

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW
ISSUE: 'Top-10 permutation features identified and interpreted' — 'interpreted' has no concrete deliverable.
QUESTION: What does interpretation look like? A table? A sentence per feature?
SUGGESTED FIX: 'Top-10 permutation features printed with feature name, importance score, and a one-line description of what the feature measures (e.g., bipolarity_score: 0.034 — completeness of construct bipolarity)'

title: Route 1 — Text-only baseline classifier
description:
## Complexity
Medium (train logistic regression, evaluate)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Train a logistic regression classifier on text embeddings only (768-dim). This is the baseline that routes 2 and 3 must beat to demonstrate graph modality signal.

### Implementation
1. Load text embeddings and split IDs
2. Train LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced', max_iter=1000, C=1.0) on train set
3. Evaluate on validation set: macro-F1, per-class F1, confusion matrix
4. Save trained model as `cache/baseline_model.joblib` using sklearn joblib dump
5. Save validation predictions to `cache/baseline_val_preds.npy`

### Tests (`tests/test_baseline.py`)
- Model trains without error
- Predictions have correct shape
- Macro-F1 is computed correctly
- Per-class F1 is >= 0 for all 3 classes
- Validation set is not leaked into training

### Acceptance criteria
- Logistic regression trained on text embeddings
- Validation macro-F1 recorded
- Confusion matrix saved
- Per-class F1 reported for all 3 cohorts
- Model saved to cache/baseline_model.joblib
- All tests pass

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW
ISSUE: 'Save trained model' — no format or path specified.
QUESTION: Save as joblib? Pickle? Where?
SUGGESTED FIX: 'Save trained model as cache/baseline_model.joblib using sklearn joblib dump'

title: Create fixed train/val/test split
description:
## Complexity
Low (sklearn train_test_split, save IDs)

## Recommended model
haiku

## Description
Create a fixed stratified 70/15/15 split (seed=42) and save split IDs. The test set must never be used for model selection or hyperparameter tuning.

### Implementation
1. Load all transcript IDs with labels (workforce=0, creatives=1, scientists=2)
2. Split: 70% train, 15% val, 15% test, stratified by label, seed=42
3. Save split IDs to `cache/split_ids.json` with format: `{train: [...], val: [...], test: [...]}`
4. Log split sizes and class distribution per split

### Acceptance criteria
- Split sizes: ~875 train, ~187 val, ~188 test (or close, given 1,250 total)
- Class distribution preserved across splits (stratified)
- Split IDs saved and never modified after creation
- Seed=42 for reproducibility

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text: CLARITY REVIEW: ✓ Clear. Split sizes and class distribution are specified. Seed pinned for reproducibility. Output format is explicit (JSON with train/val/test arrays).

title: Encode text embeddings for all transcripts
description:
## Complexity
Low (run existing script, verify output)

## Recommended model
haiku

## Description
Run `encoding/text_encoder.py` to produce 768-dim sentence-transformer embeddings for all 1,250 transcripts. Embeddings are cached to `cache/text_embeddings.npy`. Shared across all routes.

### Implementation
1. Verify `encoding/text_encoder.py` loads tagged transcripts and encodes with `all-mpnet-base-v2`
2. Run: `uv run python encoding/text_encoder.py`
3. Verify output: `cache/text_embeddings.npy` shape is (1250, 768)
4. Verify `cache/text_embedding_ids.json` has 1,250 IDs in correct order

### Tests (`tests/test_text_encoder.py`)
- Embedding matrix shape is (N, 768) where N = number of transcripts
- ID list length matches embedding count
- Embeddings are float32
- Idempotent: second run loads from cache, doesn't re-encode
- Cache files exist after first run

### Acceptance criteria
- 1,250 × 768-dim embeddings cached
- ID cache matches transcript order
- Idempotent on re-run (loads from cache)
- All tests pass

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text: CLARITY REVIEW: ✓ Clear. All acceptance criteria are measurable (shape, count, dtype, idempotency). Sort order is implicit from sorted(glob) in text_encoder.py — deterministic.

title: Phase 3 — Encoding + Classification
description:
## Epic
Encode transcripts and graphs into feature vectors, train three classification routes (text-only baseline, text + graph stats, text + GIN), and evaluate on held-out test set.

## Gate
All three routes evaluated on test set exactly once. Results logged to `results-log.md`.

## Pre-build decisions
- Split: 70/15/15 stratified, seed=42
- Text encoder: `all-mpnet-base-v2` (768-dim)
- Route 2 classifier: Logistic regression (interpretable first)
- GNN: per ENGINEERING.md defaults (GIN, lr=1e-3, 50 epochs, early stopping patience=10)
- Primary metric: macro-averaged F1

## Scope
- Encode 1,250 transcripts to 768-dim embeddings
- Compute 30-dim graph statistics per transcript
- Train GIN encoder for 128-dim graph embeddings
- Train and evaluate all three routes
- Final evaluation on held-out test set (touched once)

title: Fusion experiment — summary report
description:
## Complexity
Low (writing task, synthesize from completed experiments)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Synthesize all fusion experiment findings into a coherent report in results-log.md.

### Sections
1. Architecture: target-agnostic encoders vs conflated (old) design — why the change matters
2. GIN autoencoder results: reconstruction quality, embedding visualization (PCA/UMAP)
3. Classifier comparison: single vs stacked vs gated vs late fusion on both targets
4. Disentanglement: complementarity matrices, GRAPH-UNIQUE quantification
5. Synthesis — answers to the three gate questions:
   - (a) With target-agnostic encoders, does graph modality add signal over text alone?
   - (b) Does gated fusion outperform stacked concatenation?
   - (c) Does the answer differ by target (AI adoption vs cohort)?
6. Comparison with Phase 3 results (conflated GIN) — what changes when you freeze the encoder?
7. Recommendations for CDT modality architecture

## Acceptance criteria
- All 3 gate questions answered with quantitative evidence
- Comparison tables for all architecture x target combinations
- Added to results-log.md under 'Fusion Experiment (Target-Agnostic)' section
- Cross-referenced with Phase 3/4 results
- Updated CHARTER.md and ENGINEERING.md if architecture changes warrant it

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: ✓ Clear
Well-structured. Seven named sections, three specific gate questions, quantitative evidence required. Cross-references with Phase 3/4. No issues.

title: Disentanglement analysis — complementarity matrices
description:
## Complexity
Low (analysis on frozen embeddings + trained classifiers)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Build complementarity matrices for all architecture x modality x target combinations to answer: does adding a frozen graph modality embedding help, and which classifier architecture extracts the most complementary signal?

### Analysis
For each target (AI adoption, cohort) and each architecture (stacked, gated, late):
1. Load per-example test predictions from Bead C results
2. Build 2x2 complementarity matrix: text-only correct/wrong vs (text+graph) correct/wrong
3. Quantify: GRAPH-UNIQUE fraction, TEXT-UNIQUE fraction, overlap coefficient
4. Compare across architectures: which fusion approach captures the most complementary signal?

### Key questions
- Is the GRAPH-UNIQUE cell populated when encoders are frozen? (vs current conflated results)
- Does gated fusion increase GRAPH-UNIQUE capture vs stacked concat?
- Does the answer differ by target?

### Implementation
1. Create notebooks/05_fusion_analysis.py (Marimo notebook) or classification/fusion/analysis.py
2. Load all experiment results from Bead C
3. Build complementarity matrices, render as heatmaps
4. Summary table: architecture vs GRAPH-UNIQUE fraction by target

## Acceptance criteria
- Complementarity matrices for all architecture x target combinations
- Per-class breakdown
- Comparison table: architecture vs GRAPH-UNIQUE fraction
- Answer: does frozen graph modality add complementary signal?

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW
MINOR: File format undecided — 'notebooks/05_fusion_analysis.py (Marimo notebook) or classification/fusion/analysis.py.' The bead should commit to one. Marimo fits the project convention (notebooks 01-04 are all Marimo) and the analysis is interactive/visual (heatmaps, matrices).
SUGGESTED FIX: Commit to 'notebooks/05_fusion_analysis.py (Marimo notebook).' Marimo is the standard for analysis in this project per CLAUDE.md architecture principle 'Scripts for pipeline, Marimo for analysis.'

title: Modality embedding dataset — package frozen embeddings
description:
## Complexity
Low (extract and package existing embeddings)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Extract all three frozen modality embeddings for both targets (AI adoption n=1,224, cohort n=1,250) and package as clean .npz files with metadata. This is the single source of truth that all downstream classifiers consume.

### Implementation
1. Create encoding/build_dataset.py
2. For each transcript in the split (train/val/test):
   - Text embedding: load from cache/text_embeddings_human_only.npy (already frozen SBERT)
   - Stats embedding: compute from encoding/graph_stats.py (already deterministic)
   - GIN embedding: run frozen encoder from Bead A (new)
3. Load labels for both targets from demographics.jsonl (AI adoption) and split (cohort)
4. Save as:
   - cache/modality_dataset/ai_adoption_train.npz (embeddings + labels)
   - cache/modality_dataset/ai_adoption_val.npz
   - cache/modality_dataset/ai_adoption_test.npz
   - cache/modality_dataset/cohort_train.npz
   - cache/modality_dataset/cohort_val.npz
   - cache/modality_dataset/cohort_test.npz
5. Each .npz contains: text_emb (N,768), stats_emb (N,30), graph_emb (N,128), labels (N,), transcript_ids (N,)

## Acceptance criteria
- All 6 .npz files created with correct shapes
- Labels verified: AI adoption (602 tool_user, 622 integrated), Cohort (875 train / 187 val / 188 test)
- Loader utility: single function load_dataset(target, split) -> dict of arrays
- Metadata file documents shapes, label distributions, source encoders

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: ✓ Clear
MINOR: 'Metadata file documents shapes, label distributions, source encoders' — format not specified.
SUGGESTED FIX: 'Metadata saved as cache/modality_dataset/README.json with keys: shapes, label_distributions, source_encoders, created_at.' JSON is self-describing and machine-readable.

title: Notebook 01 -- Extraction Review
description:
## Complexity
Medium (API calls if quantitative comparison needed, otherwise low)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Create notebooks/01_extraction_review.py -- a Marimo notebook for inspecting extraction quality and model comparison results.

### Content
1. Model comparison table -- Load and display rubric-scored comparison data (R1-R5 per model per transcript). If structured rubric scores do not exist (extraction/model_comparison/results/ is empty), the notebook must clearly state this and show the qualitative validation_report.md narrative instead.
2. Qualitative graph inspection -- Interactive selector to load a graph by transcript_id and display node type counts, node labels and grounding spans, edge list with relation types, validation violations (if any).
3. Corpus-level summary -- Mean nodes/edges per cohort, violation rate, bipolarity score distribution.

### Implementation
- Use polars for tabular data
- Use marimo reactive UI for transcript selector (mo.ui.dropdown or mo.ui.text)
- Load graphs from data/graphs/free_text/ or data/graphs/canonical/
- If re-running model comparison: use extraction/model_comparison/run_comparison.py plus rubric_scorer.py to generate structured scores, then load them

## Acceptance criteria
- Notebook renders without errors via uv run marimo edit notebooks/01_extraction_review.py
- Displays either quantitative rubric comparison table or clear fallback message explaining data is unavailable
- Transcript selector loads any graph and shows node/edge summary
- Corpus-level stats match values in results-log.md (within 1 percent tolerance)
- Saved as .py file tracked in git

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: Clear with minor note
Acceptance criteria are verifiable: render check, fallback message check, transcript selector check, stats tolerance check, git tracking check. The conditional path (quantitative vs qualitative) is explicitly handled. One note: the executor should NOT re-run model comparison unless explicitly directed -- the fallback path is the default.

title: Notebook 03 — Classification Results
description:
## Complexity
Low (pure visualization, all data pre-computed)

## Recommended model
haiku

## Description
Create notebooks/03_classification_results.py -- a Marimo notebook that serves as the interactive dashboard for all classification experiments.

### Content
1. Route comparison table -- Load results/comparison_5routes_*.json and display: val macro-F1 and test macro-F1 for all 5 routes; delta vs R1 baseline; highlight best performer per metric
2. Confusion matrices -- Display PNGs from results/confusion_matrices_*.png or render heatmaps from JSON data: one matrix per route (5 total); normalized and absolute versions
3. Per-class F1 breakdown -- Bar chart per class (workforce / creatives / scientists) across routes: highlight that creatives are hardest (F1 ~0.65-0.72); scientists benefit most from graph features
4. Feature importance -- Route 2 permutation importance: top 10 features from encoding/graph_stats.py output; horizontal bar chart
5. Demographic classification -- Extend with AI adoption results: load results/demographic_classification_*.json; compare cohort classification vs AI adoption classification; show that AI adoption has more graph headroom (+4.3pp from stats)
6. Training curves -- Display cache/gnn_curves.png for Route 3/3b

### Implementation
- Load all result JSONs via json module
- Use polars for table construction
- Use matplotlib/seaborn for plots
- Marimo reactive cells for route selector (show details for selected route)

## Acceptance criteria
- Notebook renders without errors
- Route comparison table matches results-log.md exactly
- Confusion matrices display correctly (either embedded PNG or rendered heatmap)
- Per-class F1 bars show correct ordering (workforce > scientists > creatives)
- Feature importance shows diameter_norm, component_ratio, mixed_stance_frac as top 3
- Demographic section shows AI adoption text-only F1 = 0.67, graph-added +4.3pp
- Saved as .py file tracked in git

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: Clear
Lowest complexity bead with precisely pinned acceptance criteria. Specific values are referenced (diameter_norm top feature, +4.3pp graph headroom, 0.67 text F1). All data sources are named with glob patterns. Recommended model haiku is appropriate for pure visualization.

title: Notebook 02 — Graph Exploration
description:
## Complexity
Medium (statistical analysis + visualization, no API calls)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Create notebooks/02_graph_exploration.py -- a Marimo notebook for exploring concept graph topology across professional cohorts. This notebook produces the descriptive statistics that feed into Phase 4's H1-H4 confirmatory testing.

### Content
1. Cohort overview -- Side-by-side summary statistics (workforce / creatives / scientists): mean nodes, edges, constructs, values, stances, CSMs per graph; node type proportions (normalized bar charts)
2. Valence analysis -- Stance valence distribution by cohort: positive / negative / mixed / ambivalent proportions; statistical test: Kruskal-Wallis or chi-squared (preview of H2)
3. Construct quality -- Bipolarity completeness by cohort: fraction of constructs with both poles defined; mean bipolarity_score per cohort
4. Degree and centrality -- Network structure: degree distribution histograms per cohort; Value node degree (hub-and-spoke preview of H1); betweenness centrality distributions
5. Relation types -- Edge-level analysis: SERVES / EXPRESSED_VIA / MODULATED_BY / CONFLICTS_WITH proportions; CONFLICTS_WITH prevalence (rare edge type)
6. Interactive graph viewer -- Load individual graph, display NetworkX spring layout with color-coded nodes

### Implementation
- Load all canonical graphs from data/graphs/canonical/
- Use networkx for graph metrics, polars for aggregation
- Use matplotlib for static plots; marimo renders them reactively
- Include effect size previews (Cliff's delta or Cohen's d) where applicable

## Acceptance criteria
- Notebook renders without errors
- All 6 sections produce visible output
- Cohort summary statistics match values derivable from graph corpus (cross-check: mean nodes ~14.9, mean edges ~13.6)
- Valence proportions match sample data: creatives negative ~40%, scientists negative ~35%, workforce negative ~39%
- Interactive graph viewer displays at least 1 sample graph with color-coded node types
- Saved as .py file tracked in git

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: Clear
All 6 sections have concrete outputs. Cross-check values (mean nodes ~14.9, valence proportions) are pinned from corpus sampling. Effect size preview is a nice-to-have, not blocking. Interactive viewer criterion is bounded (1 sample graph). No ambiguity.

title: Notebooks 1-3: Interactive Analysis and Reporting
description:
## Epic
Create three Marimo notebooks that provide interactive entry points into the extraction, topology, and classification results. These are documentation and reproducibility artifacts — they do not produce new data, but make existing data inspectable without re-running scripts.

## Scope
- Notebook 01: Extraction quality review — model comparison scoring + qualitative graph inspection
- Notebook 02: Graph topology exploration — cohort-level structural distributions, node type counts, valence analysis
- Notebook 03: Classification results dashboard — route comparison, confusion matrices, feature importance, per-class breakdown

## Gate
All three notebooks render without errors and are tracked in git. ENGINEERING.md notebook section updated.

## Notes
- Marimo notebooks save as .py files (version-control friendly)
- No new extraction or model training — pure analysis on existing artifacts
- Notebook 02 structural metrics feed into Phase 4 H1-H4 analysis
- Notebook 01 requires model comparison re-run if quantitative rubric scores are needed; can fall back to qualitative-only using existing DeepSeek graphs

comments.author: michaelarutyunov
comments.text:
CLARITY REVIEW: Clear
Epic scope is well-bounded (3 notebooks only). Gate is measurable (render without errors + tracked in git). Notes correctly flag that notebook 02 feeds Phase 4 and notebook 01 has a fallback path. No hidden decisions.

title: Phase 1 post-mortem and Phase 4 synthesis
description:
## Complexity
Low (writing task, no code)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Two deliverables:

### Phase 1 post-mortem
Create `.claude/context/phase1-postmortem.md` — retrospective on extraction phase:
- What went well (DeepSeek reliability, prompt versioning, validation)
- What went wrong (Anthropic endpoint thinking-mode blocking, JSON truncation)
- Key decisions (OpenAI-compatible endpoint, JSON mode, three-model comparison)
- Lessons for future extraction pipelines

### Phase 4 synthesis
After H1-H4 and AI adoption analysis complete, update results-log.md with a synthesis section:
- Which hypotheses were supported?
- Does graph topology differentiate by cohort, AI adoption, both, or neither?
- What does this mean for the modality hypothesis?
- Future work and open questions

## Acceptance criteria
- phase1-postmortem.md created with all sections
- Results-log.md updated with Phase 4 synthesis
- Cross-references to results-log.md, graph-schema.md, extraction-log.md
close_reason: Created .claude/context/phase1-postmortem.md with extraction retrospective. Updated results-log.md with H1-H4 results, AI adoption analysis, and Phase 4 synthesis section.

title: Exploratory: structural analysis by AI adoption
description:
## Complexity
Medium (same metrics as H1-H4, regrouped by AI adoption)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Apply the same structural metrics from H1-H4 to the AI adoption target (tool_user vs integrated, n=1,224). This is explicitly exploratory — no pre-registered hypotheses, hypothesis-generating rather than hypothesis-testing.

### Research questions
- Do integrated users have denser AI-related subgraphs? (Construct:Value ratio around AI concepts)
- Are AI concepts more central (higher betweenness) in integrated graphs?
- Do tool_user graphs show AI at the periphery — used but not central to professional identity?
- Does graph topology predict AI adoption better than it predicts cohort?

### Metrics (same as H1-H4)
- Construct:Value ratio, Value degree centrality
- Stance valence distribution
- Construct bipolarity completeness
- CSM prevalence and subtypes
- Plus: AI-concept subgraph metrics (nodes/edges involving AI-related labels)

## Implementation
1. Extend the H1-H4 notebook with AI adoption grouping
2. Compute same metrics grouped by tool_user vs integrated
3. Run Mann-Whitney U (binary target) with effect sizes
4. Compare effect sizes: cohort vs AI adoption — which target shows stronger structural differentiation?
5. Clearly label as exploratory

## Acceptance criteria
- All H1-H4 metrics computed for AI adoption groups
- Mann-Whitney U tests with Cliff's delta effect sizes
- Comparison table: structural differentiation by cohort vs by AI adoption
- Results-log.md updated
close_reason: Exploratory AI adoption analysis added to notebooks/04_structural_analysis.py. Only C:V ratio significantly differentiates tool_user vs integrated (p=0.001, delta=-0.107). Results-log.md updated with cross-target comparison.

title: Extract demographic metadata from transcripts via LLM
description:
## Complexity
Medium-High (LLM-based extraction with reasoning traces, validation on 20 samples)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Context
The current cohort classification task is dominated by text signal (0.82 test F1 text-only). To properly test whether concept graphs constitute a structurally distinct modality, we need classification targets that are harder to infer from surface-level language — where text alone might achieve ~0.5-0.6, leaving headroom for graph features to demonstrate additive value.

Variable selection follows the criteria documented in `.claude/context/demographic-variables.md`: endogenous to interview content, graph-relevant, moderate text predictability, and natural uncertainty.

## Design
Extract two attributes from human-only transcript text using an LLM with structured reasoning traces:

### Attributes

1. **Career stage**: Inferred from explicit experience/timeline markers.
   - `early` — just starting, recently graduated, first job, junior role
   - `mid` — established professional, team member, regular contributor
   - `late` — senior, managing teams, 15+ years, mentoring, nearing retirement
   - `uncertain` — no clear timing evidence

2. **AI adoption stage**: Inferred from described AI usage patterns.
   - `novice` — rarely uses AI, just trying it out, skeptical
   - `experienced` — regular user, integrates AI into workflow, has opinions
   - `power_user` — custom workflows/pipelines, fine-tuning, API-level usage, teaches others
   - `uncertain` — ambiguous or contradictory evidence

### Output format
Each extraction must include a **reasoning trace**: the specific quote(s) from the transcript that support the inference. "uncertain" is the safe default — better missing data than guessed data.

### Test phase (this bead)
- Select 20 random transcripts (stratified across cohorts)
- Run DeepSeek vs Agnes on the same 20
- Compare agreement, uncertainty rates, reasoning quality
- Pick the better model for full-scale extraction

### Full extraction (follow-up bead)
- Run the selected model on all 1,250 transcripts
- Cache results as `cache/demographics.jsonl`
- Create new classification targets (career_stage, ai_adoption)
- Evaluate text-only, graph-only, and text+graph routes on these targets

## Acceptance criteria
- 20 transcripts extracted with both DeepSeek and Agnes
- Each extraction includes career_stage, ai_adoption, and reasoning trace
- Comparison table: model vs model (agreement %, uncertainty %, reasoning quality)
- Decision documented: which model for full extraction and why
- Prompt saved to `extraction/prompts/demographics_v1.txt`

## Notes
- See `.claude/context/demographic-variables.md` for the full variable selection rationale
- Prompt must explicitly instruct the model to ONLY use explicit evidence, never stereotype
- "uncertain" is the safe default — we'd rather have missing data than wrong data
- Gender was considered and rejected: not endogenous to work interviews, most transcripts lack evidence
- Education was deferred: too correlated with cohort, potentially revisit later

title: Create canonicalisation-specialist agent
description:
## Complexity
Low (agent definition file)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Create `.claude/agents/canonicalisation-specialist/AGENT.md` — a specialist agent that owns the canonicalisation subsystem.

### Responsibilities
1. Clustering methodology (embedding model choice, distance metric, linkage method)
2. Cluster quality assessment (coherence, size distribution, outlier detection)
3. Manual review protocol (which clusters to inspect, merge/split criteria)
4. Canonical map locking — enforces immutability post-lock
5. Vocabulary drift detection (stability check, reassignment rate)

### Key files it guards
- `canonicalisation/clusterer.py`
- `canonicalisation/canonical_map.json` (immutable post-lock)
- `canonicalisation/apply_canonical.py`

### Acceptance criteria
- Agent file created with domain knowledge from ENGINEERING.md §7
- Trigger table in CLAUDE.md updated if needed
- Agent references graph-schema.md for entity type enumeration

title: Evaluate canonicalisation quality and stability
description:
## Complexity
Medium (statistical analysis + drift check)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Evaluate the canonicalisation output for quality metrics and run a stability check by adding 100 held-out transcripts to measure cluster reassignment rate.

### Evaluation
1. **Vocabulary size check** — confirm each entity type falls within expected ranges (ENGINEERING.md §7)
2. **Coverage check** — what fraction of free-text labels mapped successfully? Any unmapped labels?
3. **Cluster coherence** — for each cluster, compute mean intra-cluster cosine similarity; flag clusters below 0.5
4. **Stability check** — hold out 100 random transcripts, cluster without them, then add them back and measure label reassignment rate. < 10% reassignment = stable vocabulary.
5. **Size distribution** — histogram of cluster sizes per entity type

### Tests (`tests/test_canonical_evaluation.py`)
- Coverage rate is above 95%
- Intra-cluster similarity mean is above 0.5
- Stability reassignment rate is below 10%
- Expected vocabulary ranges are met

### Acceptance criteria
- Evaluation report written (can be inline in output or separate markdown)
- All 5 checks produce results within expected ranges
- If stability > 10%: flag for review, document decision to proceed or re-cluster
- Tests pass

title: Apply canonical labels to all graphs
description:
## Complexity
Low (straightforward label replacement + validation)

## Recommended model
haiku

## Description
Implement `canonicalisation/apply_canonical.py` to replace free-text labels with canonical equivalents in all 1,250 graphs. Writes canonicalised graphs to `data/graphs/canonical/`.

### Implementation
1. Load `canonical_map.json`
2. For each graph in `data/graphs/free_text/`:
   - Replace each node's `label` with canonical equivalent
   - Preserve all other fields (id, type, grounding_span, edges, etc.)
   - Write to `data/graphs/canonical/{tid}.json`
3. Re-validate every canonicalised graph (structural constraints unchanged)

### Tests (`tests/test_apply_canonical.py`)
- All labels replaced with canonical equivalents
- Non-label fields preserved unchanged
- Graph structure unchanged (same node count, edge count, edge relations)
- Validator passes on canonicalised graphs
- Handles labels not in canonical_map (logs warning, keeps original)
- Output count matches input count

### Acceptance criteria
- 1,250 canonicalised graphs in `data/graphs/canonical/`
- Every label replaced with canonical equivalent
- Count matches: 1,250 input = 1,250 output
- All structural constraints still pass
- Tests pass
- Ruff clean

title: Manual review + lock canonical_map.json
description:
## Complexity
Medium (manual inspection, requires human judgment)

## Recommended model
opus

## Description
Review cluster assignments produced by clusterer.py. Inspect each cluster for semantic coherence. Merge over-split clusters and split over-merged ones. Lock `canonical_map.json` — it must never be modified after this point.

### Review protocol
1. For each entity type, print all clusters with member labels
2. Flag clusters that appear semantically incoherent
3. Flag labels that appear in the wrong cluster
4. Apply manual merges/splits
5. Write final `canonical_map.json`
6. Mark as locked (add `"locked": true` and timestamp)

### Tests (`tests/test_canonical_map.py`)
- canonical_map.json exists and is valid JSON
- All 4 entity types have entries
- No duplicate keys
- Every canonical label appears in its own values (it maps to itself)
- Locked flag is present and true
- Vocabulary sizes within expected ranges

### Acceptance criteria
- All clusters reviewed (printed for inspection)
- Merges/splits documented with rationale
- `canonical_map.json` written and marked locked
- No downstream module may modify it
- Tests pass

title: Build canonical vocabulary (clusterer.py)
description:
## Complexity
Medium (sentence-transformers + sklearn clustering)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Implement `canonicalisation/clusterer.py` to cluster free-text node labels into a canonical vocabulary.

### Implementation
1. Load all 1,250 graphs from `data/graphs/free_text/`
2. For each entity type (Construct, Value, Stance, CognitiveStyleMarker), collect all unique labels
3. Encode labels with `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim, faster than mpnet)
4. Cluster with AgglomerativeClustering (cosine distance, average linkage, distance_threshold=0.3)
5. For each cluster, select the label closest to the cluster centroid as the canonical label
6. Output `canonical_map.json`: `{free_text_label: canonical_label}`

### Tests (`tests/test_clusterer.py`)
- Clustering produces non-empty map for each entity type
- Distance threshold 0.3 produces vocabulary in expected ranges
- Cluster centroid selection picks the label closest to the centroid
- Identical labels map to the same canonical label
- Empty input (no graphs) raises a clean error
- Handles graphs with missing entity types (e.g., no CognitiveStyleMarkers)

### Acceptance criteria
- `canonical_map.json` written with entries for all 4 entity types
- Vocabulary sizes in expected ranges (ENGINEERING.md §7): Values 15-25, Constructs 30-50, Stances 20-35, CSMs 8-12
- Cluster assignments are inspectable (print sample clusters per type)
- All tests pass
- Ruff clean

title: Phase 2 — Canonicalisation
description:
## Epic
Canonicalise the 1,250 extracted concept graphs by clustering semantically similar free-text node labels into a shared vocabulary. Lock the canonical map before any downstream modelling.

## Gate
`canonical_map.json` locked and never modified post-lock. All 1,250 graphs canonicalised.

## Scope
- Cluster free-text labels per entity type using sentence-transformer embeddings + agglomerative clustering
- Manual review of cluster assignments; merge/split as needed
- Lock canonical_map.json
- Apply canonical labels to all graphs
- Tests for clusterer and apply_canonical
- Evaluation: vocabulary sizes in expected ranges, cluster quality metrics, stability check

## Recommended agent
canonicalisation-specialist (create during phase start)

title: Scale extraction — 300 transcripts
description:
## Complexity
High (batch API orchestration + error handling + quality gates + cost tracking)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Run the winning extraction model on 300 transcripts (100 per split, stratified random sample) and cache all extracted graphs.

The extractor must:
1. Load transcript IDs stratified by split: 100 workforce, 100 creatives, 100 scientists
2. For each transcript:
   a. Check cache: if `data/graphs/free_text/{tid}.json` exists, skip
   b. Parse transcript with tagger
   c. Format for extraction
   d. Call winning model API with retry (3 attempts, backoff 2s/8s/32s)
   e. Parse JSON response
   f. Run validator; log violations but continue
   g. Write validated graph to `data/graphs/free_text/{tid}.json`
3. Log all failures to `extraction/failed.txt`
4. Print progress: completed/skipped/failed out of 300
5. Support resume: re-running picks up where it left off (cache check)

**Mid-scale quality gate (at 50 transcripts):**
- Pause after 50 transcripts are cached
- Compute aggregate validation stats: % clean, mean node/edge count, bipolarity distribution
- Spot-check 5 random graphs using the same rubric as manual review (bead 1e)
- If >20% of graphs have violations OR spot-check scores drop >1 point below 1e baseline: STOP, log findings, escalate for prompt revision
- If quality passes: log checkpoint to extraction-log.md and continue

**Cost tracking (in each graph's metadata):**
- `model_used`: model identifier
- `tokens_in` / `tokens_out`: from API response
- `estimated_cost_usd`: computed from model pricing
- Cumulative cost printed every 50 transcripts; logged to extraction-log.md

Add metadata per graph-schema.md: transcript_id, split, extraction_model, prompt_version, node_count, edge_count, bipolarity_score, speaker_turns_human, speaker_turns_ai, validation_violations.

## Prerequisites
- Winning model selected (bead 1f)
- Prompt locked (bead 1e)
- Extractor in single-transcript mode (bead 1e)
- Tagger + validator working (beads 1b, 1d)

## Acceptance criteria
1. 300 graphs cached in `data/graphs/free_text/`
2. Each graph has all metadata fields including cost tracking fields
3. All graphs pass through validator; violations in metadata
4. `extraction/failed.txt` lists failures (target: <5%)
5. Re-running completes instantly (all cached)
6. Distribution: 100 workforce, 100 creatives, 100 scientists
7. Mid-scale quality gate at 50 logged to extraction-log.md
8. Cumulative cost logged to extraction-log.md
9. `uv run pytest` still passes

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: Phase 2 (canonicalisation needs all graphs)

title: Manual review — 5 transcripts
description:
## Complexity
Medium (requires API call + human judgment + iteration)

## Recommended model
opus

## Description
Extract concept graphs from 5 real transcripts using the v1 prompt, then manually review quality. This is the quality gate before model comparison.

Steps:
1. Select 5 transcripts: 2 workforce, 2 creatives, 1 scientist (mix of short and long)
2. Run extraction on each using the Anthropic API (Claude) with prompt v1
3. Run validator on each extracted graph — all must pass with zero violations
4. Manually review each graph for:
   - Are the extracted concepts genuinely present in the transcript?
   - Are the Construct bipolarities reasonable?
   - Are the relations correctly typed?
   - Are grounding spans verbatim from Human turns?
   - Are there missing concepts that should have been extracted?
5. Score each graph on the 5-criterion rubric from CHARTER.md §4 (R1–R5, score 1–3 each)
6. Record scores in `.claude/context/extraction-log.md`
7. If aggregate score < 12/15: iterate prompt (v1 → v2), re-extract all 5, re-score
8. Lock prompt version after satisfactory review

Also implement `extraction/extractor.py` in single-transcript mode:
- Load prompt from `extraction/prompts/v1.txt`
- Format transcript using `format_for_extraction()` from tagger
- Call Anthropic API with retry logic (3 attempts, backoff 2s/8s/32s)
- Parse JSON response, run validator, write to `data/graphs/free_text/{tid}.json`
- Check cache before API call
- Record `tokens_in`, `tokens_out`, `estimated_cost_usd` in graph metadata

## Prerequisites
- Dataset downloaded (bead 1a)
- Tagger working (bead 1b)
- Prompt v1 written (bead 1c)
- Validator passing (bead 1d)
- Extraction-log created (bead: 2ig)

## Acceptance criteria
1. 5 graphs extracted and cached in `data/graphs/free_text/`
2. All 5 graphs pass validation (zero violations)
3. Rubric scores recorded in extraction-log.md
4. If iterated: v2 prompt exists in `extraction/prompts/v2.txt`, v1 preserved
5. Final prompt version locked and recorded in extraction-log.md
6. `extraction/extractor.py` works in single-transcript mode with caching
7. Cost metadata (tokens_in, tokens_out, estimated_cost_usd) recorded in each graph

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: 1f (model comparison needs locked prompt)

title: Implement speaker tagger
description:
## Complexity
Medium (text parsing + dataclasses + tests)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Implement `extraction/tagger.py` — parses raw interview transcripts into structured speaker-tagged turns.

Transcripts use `Assistant:` and `Human:` prefixes on each turn. The tagger must:
1. Split transcript text into turns based on speaker prefixes
2. Map `Assistant` → `AI`, `Human` → `Human`
3. Produce a list of `Turn` dataclass instances with `speaker`, `text`, `turn_index`
4. Provide `format_for_extraction()` that formats tagged turns using `[AI]`/`[Human]` prefixes

**CRITICAL: The regex must match `Assistant:`/`Human:` only at line start.** The word "assistant" appears frequently in interview text (e.g. "I work as an assistant", "AI assistants"). A naive split on `"Assistant:"` will produce garbage.

Also implement `tests/test_tagger.py` with coverage for:
- Normal multi-turn transcript
- Single-turn edge case
- Empty transcript → returns empty list
- Transcript with only one speaker → returns only that speaker's turns
- `"Assistant:"` appearing inside Human text → does NOT split there
- Multiple consecutive turns by the same speaker → preserves both
- Whitespace-only turn text → included with stripped text
- Round-trip: parse → format preserves content

Reference implementation in ENGINEERING.md §5.1.

## Acceptance criteria
1. `extraction/tagger.py` with `Turn` dataclass, `parse_transcript()`, `format_for_extraction()`
2. `tests/test_tagger.py` with ≥8 test cases covering all scenarios above
3. `uv run pytest tests/test_tagger.py` passes
4. Tested against one real transcript from the downloaded dataset
5. Regex uses `re.MULTILINE` and requires prefix at line start (not mid-line)
6. All imports absolute from repo root

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: 1e (manual review), 1f (model comparison), 1g (scale extraction)

title: Download Anthropic Interviewer dataset
description:
## Complexity
Trivial (run existing script + quality validation)

## Recommended model
haiku

## Description
Run `uv run python data/download.py` to download the Anthropic Interviewer dataset from HuggingFace. Then validate transcript quality before any API calls are made.

The dataset: 3 CSV files — workforce_transcripts.csv (n≈1000), creatives_transcripts.csv (n≈125), scientists_transcripts.csv (n≈125). Total: ~1,250 interview transcripts.

After download, add a `validate_transcripts()` function to `data/download.py` (or a separate `data/validate.py`) that checks:
1. Every `transcript_id` is unique across all 3 CSVs
2. Every transcript has valid UTF-8 encoding
3. Every transcript has ≥1 `Assistant:` prefix and ≥1 `Human:` prefix
4. Every transcript has text length ≥ 100 characters (below this = likely corrupted)
5. No duplicate transcripts (by text content hash)
6. Print a summary report: total transcripts, min/max/median text length per split, any quality flags

## Prerequisites
- `.env` file with valid `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (for HuggingFace auth if needed)

## Acceptance criteria
1. All 3 CSV files present in `data/raw/interview_transcripts/`
2. Row counts verified: ~1000, ~125, ~125
3. Each CSV has a `transcript_id` column and a text column
4. Column schema verified by loading one CSV with polars and printing schema
5. Quality validation passes: all transcripts have speaker prefixes, valid encoding, sufficient length
6. Quality summary report printed (total transcripts, length stats per split, any flags)

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: 1b (tagger), 1c (prompt v1), 1e (manual review), 1f (model comparison), 1g (scale extraction)

title: Create extraction-log.md context doc
description:
## Complexity
Low (documentation task, no code)

## Recommended model
haiku

## Description
Create `.claude/context/extraction-log.md` — Tier 3 context doc tracking prompt version history, model comparison results, and cost tracking.

## Format
Per codified-context-principles.md §"Document structure": tables over prose, numbered rules, code blocks. Under 500 lines.

## Sections to include
1. **Prompt version table** — columns: version, date, filename, changes
2. **Model comparison results** — empty template: columns for model name, aggregate rubric score, per-criterion scores (R1–R5), notes. To be filled during bead 1f.
3. **Manual review scores** — empty template: columns for transcript_id, rubric scores (R1–R5), total, notes. To be filled during bead 1e.
4. **Quality gates** — empty template for mid-scale checkpoints: checkpoint number, transcript count, % clean, mean node/edge count, spot-check scores. To be filled during bead 1g.
5. **Cost tracking** — empty template: cumulative cost per 50-transcript batch, per-model breakdown, total budget.

## Acceptance criteria
1. `.claude/context/extraction-log.md` exists
2. Contains all 5 sections above as empty templates
3. Tables use proper markdown with headers
4. File is referenced from extraction-specialist agent Context Documents section
5. Under 100 lines (templates only; will grow during Phase 1)

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: 1e (manual review), 1g (scale extraction)
